1.pericope_label: The Death of Saul and His Sons
1.synthesis:
The battle of Jezreel turns against Israel; the men flee and fall slain on Mount Gilboa (v. 1). The Philistines press hard after Saul and kill his three sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua (v. 2). Jonathan — David’s covenant-friend, the man who had stripped himself of the royal garments for David, who had declared “you shall be king and I shall be next to you” — dies in this battle without ever seeing the fulfillment of what he had confessed. Saul is hit by archers and asks his armor-bearer to run him through so that the uncircumcised will not abuse him (vv. 3–4). The armor-bearer is afraid; Saul falls on his own sword. The armor-bearer follows his example (v. 5). When the men of Israel across the valley see that Saul and his sons are dead, they flee and abandon their cities; the Philistines come and occupy them (v. 7).

Saul’s death by his own sword completes the arc that began with the spear thrown in his house. The man who had thrown spears at David and Jonathan dies by falling on his own. The three sons who die — including Jonathan — are the completion of the divine word spoken at En-dor through Samuel: “tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me.” The death on Gilboa is the end of the Saulide monarchy and the end of the person who had been the last obstacle between David’s anointing and his coronation. The tragedy is compounded: Jonathan dies the same death as the father whose jealousy drove David into exile — the covenant-keeper and the covenant-breaker fall on the same field on the same day.
1.historical_context: Mount Gilboa is in the Jezreel Valley in the northern hill country; the Philistine army had assembled at Aphek (29:1) and advanced northward. The collapse of the Israelite forces at Gilboa enabled the Philistine occupation of the northern town network; the abandonment of cities across the Jordan valley marks the military low point of the premonarchic period. Saul’s death by falling on his sword anticipates the suicide method of other biblical figures (Ahithophel in 2 Sam 17:23; Zimri in 1 Kgs 16:18). David’s lament over Saul and Jonathan in 2 Sam 1:17–27 is the literary response to the death narrative of chapter 31.
1.literary_structure: 31:1–7 is the narrative fulfillment of Samuel’s En-dor oracle. The battle report is brief and precise: the army falls, the sons die by name (Jonathan first), Saul is wounded, requests death, falls on his sword. The narrator’s economy does not pause for grief; the deaths are noted and the consequences (city abandonment, Philistine occupation) follow immediately. The grief belongs to David’s lament in 2 Samuel 1; the 1 Samuel narrative closes with the stark record of what happened and the communal response of flight.
1.theological_application: The death of Saul on Gilboa is the completion of a trajectory that began with disobedience and was marked by every subsequent refusal of the covenant relationship. The En-dor oracle was not news at this level; the entire Samuel narrative has been moving toward this moment since the rejection at Gilgal. The tragedy is not arbitrary but is the accumulation of choices: the kingdom was Saul’s to inhabit; his fear, jealousy, and repeated disobedience produced the conditions that made Gilboa inevitable. Communities of faith can note this not as determinism but as covenantal consequence: the trajectory of a life choices takes is not reversed by momentary clarity (En-gedi, the second sparing) unless the structural pattern changes.
1.christology: The death of Jonathan — the innocent covenant-keeper who dies with the guilty king on the same field — is one of the most haunting injustices of the narrative. He who had done nothing to deserve Saul’s fate dies it anyway, simply by sharing a field, a battle, a family. Romans 5:12: “sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men.” Jonathan’s death with Saul images the innocent caught in the consequences of the guilty’s choices — the pattern that the entire human race inhabits. Christ enters this pattern not to die as the innocent caught in the guilty’s consequences but to die as the innocent who takes the guilty’s consequences deliberately, so that the covenant-keepers who would have died with Saul might live with David.

8.synthesis:
The day after the battle, the Philistines come to strip the slain and find Saul and his three sons on Mount Gilboa (v. 8). They cut off his head, strip his armor, and send messengers through all Philistia to carry the good news to the temples of their idols and to the people (v. 9). They put his armor in the temple of the Ashtaroth and fasten his body to the wall of Beth-shan (v. 10). When the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead hear what the Philistines have done to Saul, the valiant men of the city walk all night and take the bodies of Saul and his sons from the wall of Beth-shan; they burn them, bury the bones under the tamarisk tree in Jabesh, and fast seven days (vv. 11–13).

The book of 1 Samuel closes not with a theological verdict but with a burial: the men of Jabesh-gilead — who were Saul’s first beneficiaries when he led Israel to rescue them from Nahash the Ammonite (ch. 11) — walk through the night to recover their king’s body and give him honorable burial. The covenant loyalty that Jabesh-gilead owed Saul from his first military victory is paid in his death: they do not abandon him to Philistine desecration. The book that began with Samuel’s birth and a prayer for a faithful priest and a faithful king ends with a tamarisk tree in Jabesh-gilead and seven days of fasting. The king is gone; the transition to David has begun; but the last act of 1 Samuel is human faithfulness in the dark — the men who walked all night to bury their king.
8.historical_context: The display of Saul’s body on the walls of Beth-shan was a form of public humiliation practiced by victorious armies to display their conquest and demoralize the defeated. The decapitation of Saul mirrors the decapitation of Goliath by David — a symbolic reversal: David had taken the Philistine champion’s head; now the Philistines take Israel’s champion’s head. The burning of the bodies before burial was unusual in Israelite practice (normally reserved for criminals) and may reflect the condition of the bodies after exposure on the wall. The seven-day fast for Saul at Jabesh-gilead is the appropriate mourning for a fallen king; David’s own fast and lament in 2 Sam 1 continues the mourning.
8.literary_structure: The closing of 1 Samuel (31:8–13) balances the Philistine desecration with the Jabesh-gilead burial in a single unit. The movement from public dishonor (head displayed, armor in the temple) to private honor (burial, fasting) is the narrative structure of the chapter’s close. The tamarisk tree under which Saul is buried recalls the tamarisk tree under which he had been sitting when he received news of David’s location at Keilah (22:6) — the same tree species brackets his reign at its judicial midpoint and its physical end.
8.theological_application: The men of Jabesh-gilead who walk all night to bury their king demonstrate that covenant loyalty extends beyond the death of the one to whom it was owed. They did not owe Saul their lives; they owed him gratitude for the rescue of chapter 11. They repay that gratitude not with military support (which they could not provide) but with the only thing they can offer after his death: honorable burial. Communities of faith that honor those who served them even after those persons have died — maintaining memory, acknowledging debt, extending care to their survivors — practice the Jabesh-gilead loyalty. David recognizes this; when he becomes king he sends messengers to Jabesh-gilead to bless them for their loyalty to Saul (2 Sam 2:5–6).
8.christology: The burial of Saul by the faithful of Jabesh-gilead after his public display on the walls of Beth-shan anticipates the pattern of every honorable burial following public execution. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus asking Pilate for Jesus’s body (John 19:38–40) — disciples who had been secret and inadequate in their discipleship now coming forward at the moment of maximum public disgrace to give the publicly dishonored body honorable burial — follow the Jabesh-gilead pattern. The loyal care for the body of the fallen anointed one, performed at personal risk by those who owed him gratitude, is the last human act that 1 Samuel can offer before the resurrection glory of 2 Samuel’s dawn breaks. The burial is not the end; it is the preparation for what follows.
